remediation_direction: Incrementally carve domain routers out of routes.ts (payments, amenities, reconciliation, portal, admin) behind an Express Router per domain; split storage.ts into per-aggregate repositories. Do it move-only (no behavior change) with the existing tests as the safety net. No big-bang rewrite.
regression_risk: medium — mechanical extraction can drop a route registration or middleware order; mitigated by move-only commits + route-inventory diff.
tests_required: route-inventory snapshot (count + paths) before/after each extraction
existing integration suite green
limitations: Did not enumerate circular-dependency cycles statically (no madge run — read-only, no install); coupling asserted from size + single-file concentration, not a dependency graph.

title: Postgres pool has no max configured → pg default of 10 connections caps throughput under the automation sweep
summary: The single shared pg.Pool sets connectionTimeoutMillis + idleTimeoutMillis but NOT `max`, so node-postgres defaults to max=10 connections for the entire process — shared by all HTTP request handlers AND the monolithic 5-minute automation sweep that fans out many sub-sweeps.
evidence: server/db.ts:9 `export const pool = new pg.Pool({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL, connectionTimeoutMillis: 10000, idleTimeoutMillis: 30000 });` — no `max`.
impact: With fly.toml hard_limit=250 concurrent requests on one machine plus a heavy periodic sweep, 10 pooled connections is a throughput ceiling: excess queries queue up to the 10s connectionTimeout then throw. Under load or during a long sweep this surfaces as intermittent request timeouts/5xx that look random. Neon also imposes its own connection ceiling, so the number must be chosen against Neon's limit, not maximized blindly.
validation: Pool config read directly; default-max behavior is node-postgres documented default (10).
remediation_direction: Set an explicit `max` sized to Neon's connection limit for the plan (and reserve headroom for the sweep), e.g. max=15-20 with a Neon pooler (PgBouncer/`-pooler` host) in front; consider a separate small pool for the background sweep so it can't starve request handlers.
regression_risk: low — additive config; wrong (too-high) value could hit Neon's ceiling, so verify against the plan limit.
tests_required: load test at expected concurrency observing pool wait time
confirm Neon max_connections for the current plan
limitations: Did not measure live pool saturation (no prod access); impact is inferred from config + concurrency limit, not observed.

title: Six in-process setInterval schedulers + the automation sweep are guarded only by in-memory state → duplicate money actions on any multi-machine scale-out
summary: All scheduled work runs as in-process setInterval timers, and the monolithic automation sweep (autopay, delinquency notices, assessment dispatch, bank-feed sync, etc.) is deduped only by an in-memory `globalAutomationState` flag. There is no cross-process lock. Safe today only because fly.toml pins min_machines_running=1; the moment a second machine runs (scale-out, or an overlap during a rolling deploy), every timer fires on every machine.
evidence: server/index.ts ~340 `const timer = setInterval(() => { runAutomationSweep()... })`; dedupe is `globalAutomationState.__automationJobState` (in-mem, per-process). fly.toml:44 `auto_stop_machines = "stop"`, :45 `auto_start_machines = true`, :46 `min_machines_running = 1`; a fly.toml comment itself notes "For future multi-machine scale-out...". Five more setInterval schedulers at the listed locations.
impact: On >1 machine the autopay sweep double-charges owners, delinquency notices/emails double-send, and assessment dispatch duplicates — real-money and owner-facing blast radius. Even single-machine, a rolling deploy briefly runs old+new instances, so an overlapping sweep window can double-fire. This is the structural root of the 'scheduled jobs' fragility.
validation: Timers + in-mem guard read directly; scale config read from fly.toml.
remediation_direction: Move the sweep dedupe to a cross-process lock (Postgres advisory lock `pg_try_advisory_lock` around each sweep, keyed per sweep type) BEFORE any scale-out; longer term move scheduling to a durable queue/leader-election. Gate the fix so it is a no-op on single-machine.
regression_risk: medium — an advisory lock that isn't released on crash could stall a sweep; use a session-scoped lock that auto-releases on disconnect.
tests_required: two-instance test proving only one sweep runs
crash-mid-sweep releases the lock
limitations: Did not confirm whether rolling deploys actually overlap sweep windows in prod; double-fire on scale-out is deductive from in-mem-only dedupe.

title: Google Maps API key committed in fly.toml — cost/quota-theft exposure if the key is not usage-restricted
summary: A live Google Maps API key is committed in fly.toml (`VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIza..."`). Because it's a VITE_ variable it ships to the browser bundle anyway (not a secret leak), but a committed, client-exposed Maps key that lacks HTTP-referrer + API restrictions is directly abusable → third-party quota consumption billed to the account.
evidence: fly.toml:32 `VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIzaSyCsb1tCLccLzdaKgCm4263A32S0Z0LvdR8"`.
impact: If unrestricted, any actor can pull the key from the JS bundle and drive Maps API calls on William's billing — an unbounded cost meter. Also in git history, so rotation requires a real key roll, not just a value change.
validation: Key presence confirmed in fly.toml; restriction status NOT verified (requires Google Cloud console access, out of read-only scope).
remediation_direction: In Google Cloud console: restrict the key to (1) the production HTTP referrer(s) and (2) only the Maps JS/Places APIs actually used; then rotate the committed key (it is in git history) and inject the new one as a Fly secret consumed at build. Add a quota cap alert.
regression_risk: low — referrer restriction can break local/preview origins if too narrow; include dev origins.
tests_required: confirm map still renders from the prod origin after restriction
confirm a non-allowed origin is rejected
limitations: Cannot see the key's current restriction config from the repo; severity is conditional on it being unrestricted.

title: Low explicit-index-to-table ratio (47 indexes / 183 tables) suggests missing composite indexes on hot multi-tenant query paths
summary: schema.ts declares 47 explicit index() definitions across 183 tables (~0.26 per table). For a multi-tenant financial app whose hot queries filter by association_id + status + date, that ratio hints many tables rely on the PK only, leaving common filtered/sorted reads to sequential scans as data grows.
evidence: `rg -c 'index(' shared/schema.ts` = 47; `grep -c pgTable( shared/schema.ts` = 183.
impact: As per-association row counts grow (ledger entries, transactions, notices), unindexed association_id+status filters degrade to seq scans → slow dashboards and slow sweeps (compounding PERF-B-002's pool pressure). Financial reads are the hottest path.
validation: Ratio computed; SPECIFIC missing indexes NOT confirmed — no EXPLAIN/query-plan access in read-only scope.
remediation_direction: Capture pg_stat_statements from prod, EXPLAIN the top filtered queries (association_id/status/date), and add composite indexes where a seq scan on a large table appears. Add indexes migration-by-migration, verify each with EXPLAIN.
regression_risk: low — additive indexes; watch write amplification on very hot insert tables.
tests_required: EXPLAIN before/after on the top-5 filtered queries
pg_stat_statements top-query capture
limitations: This is a heuristic signal (ratio), not a proven missing-index list; needs runtime query-plan verification to promote to validated.

title: Database seeding runs on every process boot (await seedDatabase) — boot latency + correctness risk if the seed is not fully idempotent
summary: server/index.ts awaits seedDatabase() during startup. seed.ts is 4,257 lines. With Fly auto_stop/auto_start, a cold start pays the full seed cost before the server accepts traffic, and any non-idempotent seed step could mutate prod data on each restart.
evidence: server/index.ts:564 `await seedDatabase();` inside the boot path; seed.ts = 4257 lines.
impact: Cold-start latency after auto_stop (first request after idle waits for seed); if any seed write is not guarded by an existence check, restarts could re-insert/reset data. On a live customer's books that is a data-integrity risk, not just a perf one.
validation: Boot-path call confirmed; seed idempotency NOT verified line-by-line (4257 lines, out of proportional read-only budget).
remediation_direction: Gate seedDatabase() behind an env flag (seed only in dev/CI, never on prod boot), or assert every seed write is upsert/guard-checked; move seeding to an explicit one-shot migration step rather than the request-serving boot path.
regression_risk: low — flag-gating is additive; ensure prod still has its required reference rows via migration.
tests_required: boot with seed disabled still serves
re-running seed twice is a no-op (row counts stable)
limitations: Did not read all 4257 seed lines; idempotency assumed-uncertain, hence low+needs-verification.

title: POSITIVE / hypothesis-refuted: money-moving Stripe raw-fetch calls DO carry Idempotency-Key (no double-charge gap found)
summary: The mapper's risk #2 hypothesised idempotency gaps in the raw-fetch (no-SDK) Stripe calls. Verified the money-moving POSTs and every one applies an Idempotency-Key: checkout/sessions (payment-service:198 + routes:6222 passthrough), off-session payment_intents (payment-service:653), and the amenity gateway (:90, keyed by caller). The double-charge-on-retry risk is largely refuted for the money paths inspected.
evidence: payment-service.ts:653 `intentHeaders["Idempotency-Key"] = offSessionChargeKey(params.transactionId)`; :198 checkout key; amenity-stripe-gateway.ts:90 `if (idempotencyKey) h["Idempotency-Key"] = idempotencyKey`; routes.ts:6222 `stripeHeaders["Idempotency-Key"] = paymentLinkCheckoutKey(...)`.
impact: Reduces the payment-integrity risk surface: retries of these charges return the original intent rather than re-charging.
validation: Read each POST's header construction directly.
remediation_direction: Keep the safety centralized: the amenity gateway only sets the key IF the caller passes one (`if (idempotencyKey)`) — make the key non-optional / assert-present at the fetch wrapper so a future call site can't silently omit it. That's the only residual: idempotency is per-call-site convention, not enforced by a shared client.
regression_risk: low
tests_required: retry-returns-same-intent test per money POST
limitations: Did not exhaustively audit every one of the 8 stripe fetch sites' headers — verified the money-moving ones; read-only usage-reconcile/search calls are non-mutating.

title: POSITIVE: bank-feed reconciliation matcher is well-designed (integer cents, confidence-scored, conflict-safe) — risk #8 largely holds up
summary: The reconciliation auto-matcher (mapper risk #8) does money math in integer cents (amountCents, Math.round(dollars*100)), scores matches 0..1 across amount/date/payor signals, only auto-applies above AUTO_MATCH_THRESHOLD=0.85, surfaces borderline candidates to the admin UI, and has an explicit conflict rule when multiple ledger entries exceed the threshold. Correctness posture is sound.
evidence: auto-matcher.ts header comment (:19-47) documents confidence tiers + conflict rule; :527 `const creditAbsCents = Math.abs(credit.amountCents)`; :546 `Math.round(Math.abs(entry.amount) * 100)`.
impact: Auto-application is bounded (threshold + conflict rule + human review of borderline), so silent mis-postings are unlikely.
validation: Read the matcher's thresholds + cents handling directly.
remediation_direction: Minor: the ledger side stores amount as a float dollars field (`entry.amount`) converted to cents via Math.round while the bank side is native integer amountCents — two money representations in one domain. Standardize on integer cents end-to-end to remove the conversion seam.
regression_risk: low
tests_required: property test: round-trip dollars↔cents never drifts a penny across the match range
limitations: Reviewed matcher thresholds + a sample of the money-math sites, not all 1272 lines of auto-matcher.ts nor the posting side end-to-end.

title: Money-moving multi-row writes are NOT transactional — server uses db.transaction exactly once (a non-money RAG path), so a mid-operation failure leaves partial financial state
summary: Across the entire server, drizzle db.transaction( is called exactly ONCE and only in the non-money RAG ingester (server/services/rag/ingester.ts); there is no raw BEGIN/COMMIT or withTransaction wrapper anywhere else. So every multi-write money operation runs as a sequence of independent awaits with no atomic boundary. Clearest instance: importBankStatementRecord (storage.ts:11148) loops over parsed bank transactions and creates each owner-ledger money entry via createOwnerLedgerEntry (storage.ts:11276) one await at a time; a failure/crash/rolling-restart partway through the loop leaves a partially-imported statement (some ledger rows written, some not, no rollback). Route-level ledger posts (routes.ts:6668/7004/15616) are likewise single-write, un-transactioned.
evidence: storage.ts:11276 'const created = await this.createOwnerLedgerEntry({...})' inside the per-row loop of 'private async importBankStatementRecord(' at storage.ts:11148, with no enclosing db.transaction; repo-wide grep of server/ for the drizzle transaction call returns exactly 1 hit, in server/services/rag/ingester.ts (non-money).
impact: Partial financial writes on any mid-operation failure (error, OOM, or the SCALE-B-003 single-machine restart) leave inconsistent owner ledgers / assessment balances requiring manual reconciliation. Blast radius = owner-ledger correctness (the delinquency + payment source-of-truth). Mitigated for RE-RUNS by the per-row referenceId idempotency check (storage.ts:11259-11273 skips already-imported rows), but NOT for the partial-state window between the failure and the re-run.
validation: Source-confirmed in a clean origin/main worktree: the single db.transaction site, the un-wrapped import loop, and the 3 route callers all read directly. The runtime frequency of partial-failure is the hypothesis half — needs fault-injection to quantify.
remediation_direction: Wrap each multi-row money operation (bank-statement import loop; any create-charge + post-ledger + update-balance sequence) in a single drizzle db.transaction so it commits atomically or rolls back; keep the per-row idempotency for re-run safety. A shared postToLedger(tx) helper would make new call sites inherit atomicity.
regression_risk: low-medium — wrapping sequential writes in a transaction changes failure semantics to all-or-nothing (the intent); must confirm no long-running import loop holds a tx open past Neon statement/idle timeout (batch large imports).
tests_required: fault-injection: fail mid bank-statement import, assert zero partial ledger rows (rollback)
re-run idempotency still holds after a rolled-back import
limitations: Read-only, no runtime/prod DB — partial-failure blast radius quantified from source structure, not observed in production.

title: ESLint scope is client-only — entire server/ (38.5k lines, all money/tenant/webhook logic) is never linted
summary: The lint script is `"lint": "eslint client/src"` — it scans ONLY client/src. The entire server tree (server/routes.ts 20,553 lines, server/storage.ts 18,036 lines, all payment/webhook/tenant-isolation code) receives zero ESLint analysis.
evidence: package.json:11  "lint": "eslint client/src",
impact: The highest-risk code in the codebase (money math, tenant scoping, webhook handling) has no lint pass — no-unused-vars, no-floating-promises, no-shadow, exhaustive-switch, etc. never run there. Bugs a linter would catch (unawaited promises in money paths, dead branches) ship unflagged.
validation: Confirmed by reading package.json scripts; no server-side lint invocation exists in package.json or CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml runs `npm run lint` which is client-only).
remediation_direction: Extend the lint script to `eslint client/src server shared` (or add a `lint:server`), add a flat eslint.config for server with @typescript-eslint's no-floating-promises + no-misused-promises rules; wire into ci.yml. Expect a large initial finding count — triage, don't bulk-suppress.
regression_risk: Low (adding lint coverage; enabling rules incrementally avoids a merge wall).
limitations: Did not run eslint against server/ to quantify the latent finding count (read-only, no install).

title: 20.5k-line routes.ts + 18k-line storage.ts monoliths with 200-315-line handlers
summary: server/routes.ts is 20,553 lines and server/storage.ts is 18,036 lines — two files hold ~38.5k lines (20% of the ~192k-line TS codebase). Individual handlers are oversized: safeInvalidateAlertCache (315 lines from :38), the payments webhook handler (223 lines from :6358), the portal board dashboard (279 lines from :14884), executive/sync (218 lines from :16217).
evidence: `wc -l server/routes.ts` -> 20553; `wc -l server/storage.ts` -> 18036; largest blocks measured by handler-boundary scan (315/279/223/218 lines).
impact: Review-miss risk (a reviewer cannot hold a 20k-line file in context — a tenant-scope omission on one of ~515 routes is easy to miss); high merge-conflict rate (parallel builders touching routes.ts collide); slow editor/LSP; the file is the single largest correctness-risk surface in the app.
validation: Line counts + handler-boundary measurement on origin/main HEAD 1e6f941.
remediation_direction: Split routes.ts by domain into server/routes/<domain>.ts registrars (payments, portal, admin, webhooks, tenant) — the repo already has a server/routes/ dir with some split files, so continue the pattern; extract the 200-315-line handlers into named service functions. Mechanical, incremental, low-behavior-change.
regression_risk: Medium (moving handlers risks middleware-order or path drift; do per-domain PRs with route-inventory diffs).
tests_required: route-inventory snapshot test (assert the full path list is unchanged after each split)
limitations: Handler line-counts are approximate (regex boundary scan, not AST).

title: npm-audit CI gate fails on CRITICAL CVEs only — high/moderate vulns merge freely
summary: security.yml self-documents 'fails on critical CVEs only. High/moderate severities are surfaced via PR label so authors can [decide].' A high-severity dependency CVE is labeled, not blocked — it can merge and auto-deploy to prod.
evidence: .github/workflows/security.yml:2 '# Runs on every PR targeting main; fails on critical CVEs only.'; :46 `CRIT=$(echo "$AUDIT" | jq '.metadata.vulnerabilities.critical // 0')` (only critical drives the gate).
impact: High-severity vulns (e.g. an auth-bypass or RCE rated High not Critical) in a dependency can land + auto-deploy (see CQ-005 auto-deploy). Combined with client-exposed money flows, the risk window is real.
validation: Read the full workflow logic; only the critical count is used in the fail condition.
remediation_direction: Escalate the gate to fail on high+critical (`npm audit --audit-level=high`), keep moderate as a label; add an allowlist file for accepted advisories so the gate stays actionable.
regression_risk: Low-medium (may block merges until existing high CVEs are resolved/allowlisted — do a one-time triage first).
limitations: Did not enumerate current npm-audit output (no install).

title: Single self-hosted macOS runner is the sole CI + deploy host — SPOF observed offline blocking all CI
summary: All four ci.yml jobs (tsc+lint, build, vitest, Playwright) and fly-deploy run on `runs-on: [self-hosted, macOS, ARM64]` — one physical Mac. If it is offline/asleep/network-flapping, ALL YCM CI and deploys stop.
evidence: ci.yml lines 52/64/75/86 all `runs-on: [self-hosted, macOS, ARM64]`.
impact: Directly observed: during founder-os#10172 (2026-07-09) the mac-ycm runner went offline mid-run with 'the self-hosted runner lost communication with the server' and 'Could not resolve host: github.com', blocking CI on an unrelated PR. No redundant runner means every such blip halts the merge/deploy pipeline for the whole product.
validation: Confirmed in ci.yml + first-hand from the #10172 run logs (job 86192740573).
remediation_direction: Register a second self-hosted runner (a Mini backup — note mini-ycm now exists per founder-os#10197) OR keep the OP #83 hosted-fallback armed so a runner-down flips back to ubuntu-latest. Add a runner-liveness monitor.
regression_risk: Low (adding redundancy).
limitations: Runner host physical availability can't be audited from the repo; evidence is the workflow config + the observed #10172 outage.

title: Every push to main auto-deploys to prod with no staging/promotion gate in the pipeline
summary: fly-deploy.yml triggers `on: push: branches:[main]` and deploys to the prod Fly app on every merge. There is no staging-promotion gate in the deploy path — a merged PR ships straight to production.
evidence: .github/workflows/fly-deploy.yml:1 '# Auto-deploy to Fly on every push to the default branch.'; :14-16 `on: push: branches: [main]`.
impact: A bad merge (a Playwright/visual regression that's non-required per CQ-006, or a high CVE per CQ-003) reaches real users immediately. No canary, no manual promote. Combined with 0 required status checks (branch protection required_checks=null), only a 1-person review stands between a merge and prod.
validation: Read the deploy workflow trigger + branch protection (required_checks=null, review=1).
remediation_direction: Gate prod deploy behind a manual promotion (environment protection rule / workflow_dispatch) OR require the yourcondomanager-staging app to pass a smoke check before the prod job runs. A staging app now exists (yourcondomanager-staging) — wire it as the gate.
regression_risk: Low (adds a gate; existing OP #31 'deploy-on-merge' intent can be preserved via auto-promote-after-staging-green).
tests_required: post-deploy smoke test as the promotion gate
limitations: Whether an out-of-band manual staging step exists operationally isn't visible from the repo.

title: Playwright (incl. visual-regression + axe a11y) is a NON-required check — PRs can merge with it red
summary: Branch protection on main has `required_status_checks: null` — no check is required, so the Playwright job (route-mock + real-backend + axe accessibility + visual regression) is advisory only. A PR with failing E2E, failing visual diff, or failing a11y can be merged.
evidence: `gh api .../branches/main/protection` -> {"required_checks":null,"review":1}; ci.yml:86 defines the Playwright job but it gates nothing.
impact: E2E regressions on money/portal/auth flows, visual regressions, and WCAG-AA a11y failures can land undetected. The most integration-representative tests in the suite have no enforcement — they rely on a human noticing the red X.
validation: Confirmed via branch-protection API + the CQ-004 Playwright flake shows how often it's red (weakening the human-noticing safeguard further).
remediation_direction: Make the deterministic Playwright projects (route-mock + a11y) required checks; keep the flaky CDN-dependent + visual projects advisory until the browser-cache hardening (founder-os#10172) stabilizes them, then promote.
regression_risk: Low-medium (requiring a flaky check would block merges — hence stabilize/split first).
limitations: Did not measure the current Playwright pass rate quantitatively.

title: Type-safety escape hatches concentrated in the money/tenant-critical files (37 in routes.ts, 14 in storage.ts)
summary: `as any` / @ts-ignore / @ts-expect-error appear 37 times in server/routes.ts and 14 times in server/storage.ts — the two files that hold payment, ledger, and tenant-scoping logic. Each occurrence defeats the type checker exactly where correctness matters most.
evidence: `grep -cE 'as any|@ts-ignore|@ts-expect-error' server/routes.ts` -> 37; same on storage.ts -> 14.
impact: A `as any` on a Stripe amount, a ledger row, or an association-scope object silently disables the compile-time guarantee that would catch a shape/units mismatch — the class of bug most dangerous in money + multi-tenant code.
validation: grep counts on origin/main; did not classify each site by risk (money vs benign).
remediation_direction: Inventory the 51 sites, prioritize those on money/tenant/ledger types, replace with proper typing or narrow type guards; add an eslint rule (once CQ-001 lands) to ban new `as any` in server/ money modules.
regression_risk: Low per-site (typing fixes); collectively M.
limitations: Counts include benign uses (e.g. test doubles); a per-site triage is needed to separate risky from harmless.

title: Stripe money calls use 6+ hand-rolled raw fetch() to api.stripe.com (no SDK) — repeated fragile abstraction
summary: Stripe is called via raw `fetch('https://api.stripe.com/v1/...')` in at least 6 places across 4 files, each re-implementing auth header, body encoding, error handling, idempotency, and retry independently — no shared client or SDK.
evidence: routes.ts:6227 checkout/sessions; routes.ts:16232 generic `/v1${path}` passthrough; storage.ts:486 /v1/account; payment-service.ts:198 & :486; stripe-connect.ts:211 — all raw `await fetch(...api.stripe.com...)`.
impact: Divergent idempotency-key handling across sites risks double-charges/double-refunds; amount-math and currency handling are re-done per site; a Stripe API change must be patched in 6 places. The generic `/v1${path}` passthrough at routes.ts:16232 is an especially broad, hard-to-audit surface. (Cross-refs Lane-B/mapper risk #2.)
validation: grep of raw Stripe fetch call-sites; did not trace idempotency-key presence per site (Lane B depth).
remediation_direction: Introduce ONE internal stripeFetch() wrapper (or the official SDK) enforcing idempotency-key, retry-with-backoff, and error normalization; migrate the 6 sites to it. Display-only per money-safety rule — no calculation change.
regression_risk: Medium (touching money call-sites — do behind tests + TEST-mode verification).
tests_required: idempotency-key presence test per money mutation
retry/timeout behavior test on the wrapper
limitations: Read-only: did not execute; idempotency correctness per-site is a Lane-B runtime concern.

title: Server error reporting is conditional on SENTRY_DSN and silently no-ops when unset
summary: observability.ts wires @sentry/node only when SENTRY_DSN is set; when absent it logs '[observability] SENTRY_DSN not set; server-side error reporting disabled' and no-ops. If the prod Fly secret is unset (the dispatch reports empty DSNs), production server errors go entirely unreported.
evidence: observability.ts:43 `dsn: process.env.SENTRY_DSN ?? null`; :59 log 'SENTRY_DSN not set; server-side error reporting disabled'.
impact: Silent prod errors — no alerting on 500s, unhandled rejections, money-path failures. Combined with auto-deploy-to-prod (CQ-005) and non-required E2E (CQ-006), a regression can reach users AND fail invisibly.
validation: Code path confirmed; the prod secret's presence CANNOT be verified from the repo (read-only, no prod creds) — hence 'needs runtime verification'. The dispatch asserts empty DSNs.
remediation_direction: Verify SENTRY_DSN is set as a Fly secret on the prod app; add a boot-time assertion that fails the deploy (or loudly warns) if unset in production; consider a startup healthcheck that surfaces 'observability disabled'.
regression_risk: Low.
tests_required: boot-time config assertion test for prod env
limitations: Cannot read prod Fly secrets; the 'empty DSN in prod' claim is inherited from the dispatch, not independently confirmed.

title: No systematic per-call-site tenant-isolation test coverage across ~515 routes
summary: Tenant/authorization tests exist for targeted areas (financial-security, plaid-route-security, alerts-mutation-security, disbursement-service) but there is no systematic per-route assertion that every one of the ~515 routes enforces association scoping. The mapper's risk #1 (assertResourceScope passes when the association is unresolved) has no dedicated regression test.
evidence: Targeted security test files present (see locations); no route-inventory-driven tenant-scope test found; ~515 routes in the 20.5k-line routes.ts.
impact: A new route that forgets assertResourceScope, or one where the association silently resolves to undefined, would leak cross-tenant data and no test would catch it. Test coverage is spot, not systematic, on the single highest-severity risk class.
validation: Enumerated the security test files; did not build the full route inventory vs test cross-map (Lane-B depth) — hence 'candidate' + moderate.
remediation_direction: Add a route-inventory-driven meta-test: enumerate all mutating routes, assert each is wrapped by an auth+scope middleware (or is on an explicit allowlist). Add a regression test for the assertResourceScope-unresolved-association case.
regression_risk: Low (adding tests).
tests_required: route-inventory tenant-scope coverage meta-test
assertResourceScope unresolved-association regression test
limitations: Full route-to-test cross-mapping not built here; coordinate with the security lane (risk #1).

title: test-routes.ts is correctly DOUBLE-gated and unreachable in production (validated-safe)
summary: The dispatch flagged test-routes.ts prod-reachability as a risk. Verified SAFE: registerTestRoutes() is imported+called only inside a gated block (index.ts:500-507), isTestModeEnabled() requires NODE_ENV=test AND PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE=1 (test-routes.ts:23), registration refuses otherwise (:51), and each handler re-checks the gate. Production cannot reach these endpoints.
evidence: test-routes.ts:23 `return process.env.NODE_ENV === 'test' && process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_TEST_MODE === '1'`; :51 `if (!isTestModeEnabled()) return;`; index.ts:500 comment 'gated at *call time*: registerTestRoutes() refuses to register'.
impact: None — reported as a positive/no-risk confirmation so the risk can be closed rather than re-audited.
validation: Read both the gate definition and the mount site; double gate (registration-time + per-handler) confirmed.
remediation_direction: No action required. Optionally add a CI assertion test that isTestModeEnabled() is false under NODE_ENV=production to lock the invariant.
regression_risk: None.
tests_required: invariant test: registerTestRoutes is a no-op under NODE_ENV=production
limitations: None.

title: Google Maps API key committed in fly.toml (version control + git history)
summary: fly.toml:32 hardcodes `VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIzaSy..."` in the committed [env] block. VITE_-prefixed vars are bundled into the client (public by design for Maps JS), but committing it puts it in git history and makes rotation a code change; exploitability depends on HTTP-referrer restriction (not verifiable from the repo).
evidence: fly.toml:32 `VITE_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY = "AIzaSyCsb1tCLccLzdaKgCm4263A32S0Z0LvdR8"`.
impact: If the key is not HTTP-referrer-restricted in Google Cloud console, it can be scraped and used to run up billing on William's Maps quota. Even if restricted, committing it in git history is poor hygiene (rotation requires a commit; the key is permanently in history).
validation: Key string confirmed in fly.toml on origin/main. Referrer-restriction status is a Google-console fact not auditable from the repo.
remediation_direction: Confirm the key is HTTP-referrer-restricted to the prod domain(s) in Google Cloud console; rotate it (it's in git history); keep client-exposed keys restricted rather than moving to a secret (Maps JS keys must be client-visible). Document the restriction as the control.
regression_risk: Low (rotation + restriction).
limitations: Cannot verify Google-console referrer restrictions from the repo — the real severity hinges on that.

title: 29 TODO/FIXME/XXX markers accumulating, concentrated in a few client files
summary: 29 TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers across server+client, concentrated in onboarding-invite.tsx (9 XXX), phone-formatter.ts (6 XXX), breadcrumb-paths.ts (6 TODO). A handful sit in money-adjacent server services (payment-receipt-email, bank-feed-sync, resale-certificate).
evidence: `grep -rInE 'TODO|FIXME|HACK|XXX|@ts-ignore|@ts-expect-error'` -> 29 total; breakdown by file per the grep uniq counts.
impact: Low individually, but 9 XXX in the onboarding-invite path and TODOs in bank-feed-sync (reconciliation) can hide unfinished edge-case handling. Accumulation without triage erodes the signal value of the markers.
validation: grep counts on origin/main; did not read each TODO's content for severity.
remediation_direction: Triage the server-side money/reconciliation TODOs (bank-feed-sync.ts, payment-receipt-email.ts) into tracked issues; convert or resolve the onboarding-invite XXX cluster.
regression_risk: None (bookkeeping).
limitations: Marker content not individually classified for real vs stale.
